{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middle[[Category:middle]] of the 3rd century to the middle[[Category:middle]] of the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middle[[Category:middle]] of the 3rd century to the middle[[Category:middle]] of the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middle[[Category:middle]] of the 3rd century to the middle[[Category:middle]] of the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middle[[Category:middle]] of the 3rd century to the middle[[Category:middle]] of the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Koboyama Kofun zenkei-2.JPG|Overview File:Koboyama Kofun funcho.JPG|location of the burial chamber File:Koboyama Kofun zenkei.JPG|Panoramic view </gallery>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Koboyama Kofun zenkei-2.JPG|Overview File:Koboyama Kofun funcho.JPG|location of the burial chamber File:Koboyama Kofun zenkei.JPG|Panoramic view </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nagano)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Koboyama Kofun}} *[http://youkoso.city.matsumoto.nagano.jp/special1_index-id_60-htm Matsumoto city tourist information official site] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190504160450/http://youkoso.city.matsumoto.nagano.jp/special1_index-id_60-htm |date=2019-05-04 }} {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.matsumoto.nagano.jp/smph/miryoku/bunkazai/takara/kuni/shiseki/koubouyamakohun.html Cultural properties of Matsumoto city]{{in lang|ja}}

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Koboyama Kofun zenkei-2.JPG|Overview File:Koboyama Kofun funcho.JPG|location of the burial chamber File:Koboyama Kofun zenkei.JPG|Panoramic view </gallery>

It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mount in Matsumoto, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft}} | name = Kōbōyama Kofun | native_name =弘法山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 弘法山古墳.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption =Kōbōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kōbōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|12|48|N|137|58|58|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsumoto, Nagano]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Chūbu region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid 3rd to early 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kōbōyama Kofun'''|弘法山古墳|}} is a large ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nakayama neighborhood of the city of [[Matsumoto, Nagano]] in the [[Chūbu region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138547|title=弘法山古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Kōbōyama Kofun is located at an elevation of 650 meters on the northern end of a hill named "Kōbōyama", to the south of the urban center of Matsumoto city. It is a "two conjoined rectangles" type tumulus ({{nihongo|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft}}''|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}}) and is aligned northwest along the ridgeline of the hill. The tumulus has a total length of 66 meters, with a posterior portion 33 meters square by six meters high, and an anterior front portion 22 meters square by two meters high. The presence of some ''[[fukiishi]]'' has been discovered, but there is no trace of any ''[[haniwa]]'' or a moat. The tomb is estimated to date from the middleof the 3rd century to the middleof the 4th century AD. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It was long known that there was an ancient tumulus at this location, and during [[World War II]], an anti-aircraft battery was installed on its summit. However, it attracted little scholarly attention after the war, and it was assumed to be of little importance. When the Matsusho Gakuen school acquired the property in 1974 and started the construction of a new school building, the true shape and size of the tumulus was first realized, and its dating to the 3rd century, or early Kofun period, made it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped tombs in eastern Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tomb was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1974, during which a gravel pit [[burial chamber]]. The burial chamber was orthogonal to the main axis of the mound. Its internal dimensions were 5.5 meters long by 1.3 meters wide and 0.9 meters high. It was unusual in that its inner walls and floor were made of stacked river stones instead of cut-and-dressed stone. In the center was the remnants of a box-shaped wooden [[sarcophagus]]. [[Grave goods]] included a Chinese-made [[Shinju-kyo|bronze mirror]], three iron swords, a copper sheet, 738 small glass balls, ''[[magatama]]'', an iron cauldron, 24 iron spearheads, and a large jug with an S-shaped mouth and many pottery shards. Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Koboyama Kofun zenkei-2.JPG|Overview File:Koboyama Kofun funcho.JPG|location of the burial chamber File:Koboyama Kofun zenkei.JPG|Panoramic view </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nagano)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

Many of these artifacts are now on display at the {{nihongo|'''Matsumoto Municipal Archaeological Museum'''|松本市立考古博物館|Matsumoto-shiritsu Kōko Hakubutsukan}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The identity of the inhabitant of the tomb is unknown, but its style is similar to tombs found in the late [[Yayoi period]] in either western Japan, or the [[Nōbi Plain]] area of [[Tōkai region]] of Japan, both areas of which mentioned as one of the kingdoms of the [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] in the Chinese [[Records of the Three Kingdoms]] as rivals to the power of the Kingdom of [[Yamatai]]. It is also theorized that this is the tomb of one of the Suwa ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', local kings of the region who are the ancestors of the [[Suwa clan]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The view from the tumulus extends to the [[Hida Mountains]] in the distance, and around the base of the hill on which the tumulus was constructed, the remains of 70 hectares of Kofun-period [[paddy field]]s to the north and 40 hectares to the east have been found. The ''kofun'' and its surroundings now have been planted with over 2000 [[Prunus × yedoensis|Yoshino sakura]] trees, and are a popular flower-viewing spot within Matsumoto city. It is approximately 15 minutes on foot from [[Matsumoto Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
